request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"path::social_communication","name":"path","one_liner":"Path was a mobile social networking app that limited sharing to close friends and family, positioning itself as a private, intimate alternative to Facebook before shutting down in 2018.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"The new pitch is ad-supported B2C, while Path's monetization per Trusted facts is subscription_recurring. Both are B2C consumer social apps, but the revenue model diverges significantly — Path never built a sustainable ad business, whereas the new pitch depends entirely on ad revenue from a niche (college towers) audience."},"market":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both target consumer social networking on mobile. Path focused on intimate sharing with close contacts globally; the new pitch targets a hyperlocal college demographic. Both compete in the crowded consumer social space where network effects determine survival."},"gtm":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both relied on organic/viral growth among a defined social cluster — Path launched via Silicon Valley early adopters and Facebook alumni networks; the new pitch targets viral spread within college residential towers. Both lack a clear paid acquisition strategy and depend on word-of-mouth within a bounded community."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Path raised ~$66M+ across multiple rounds and reached millions of users before failing; the new pitch appears to be at early/pre-scale stage targeting a single venue type. The scale ambitions differ substantially, though both started with a tight community before attempting broader expansion."}},"why_similar":"Both are consumer-facing mobile social apps built around a constrained sharing format — Path limited friend count to enforce intimacy; the new pitch limits to 30-second voice notes from a single recurring location. Both pursue virality within a bounded community (inner circle vs. college towers) and both occupy the hyper-competitive consumer social networking sector. Each attempts product differentiation (format or privacy constraints) rather than platform scale as an initial moat.","where_diverged":"1. Monetization: Path used subscription_recurring per Trusted facts; the new pitch is explicitly ad-supported, which changes CAC/LTV dynamics and requires scale that a college-tower niche may not provide. 2. Content format: Path was photo/messaging-centric; the new pitch is voice-note-only (30 seconds), a meaningfully different media type. 3. Geographic scope: Path targeted global audiences (eventually dominated by Indonesia); the new pitch is hyperlocal by design (single recurring location per user). 4. Privacy posture: Path emphasized intimacy and restricted networks; the new pitch's viral college dynamic implies public or semi-public sharing within a building community.","failure_causes":["inability to differentiate sustainably from Facebook and Instagram","privacy violations and FTC fine eroding user trust","abandonment of core product constraint (friend limit removed, diluting value proposition)","no clear path to sustainable monetization","dependence on Facebook social graph access, which was revoked","geographic concentration (Indonesia) without local monetization infrastructure","acquisition by Kakao failing to revive growth"],"lessons_for_input":["Never abandon your core product constraint — Path killed its own differentiator by removing the friend limit; protect the 30-second/location rule as a feature, not a limitation to apologize for.","Build monetization before scale, not after — ad-supported models require large, engaged audiences; validate CPM or sponsorship revenue early within your college cohort before expanding.","Respect user data and privacy from day one — Path's unauthorized contact harvesting triggered an FTC fine and lasting trust damage; bake privacy compliance into your MVP.","Do not build growth loops that depend on a platform you don't control — Path lost Facebook 'Find Friends' access overnight; own your distribution or diversify it early.","Hyperlocal virality is a launchpad, not a moat — Path's Indonesia traction couldn't be monetized at scale; map out how college-tower virality translates to a sustainable, broader network before raising a Series A."]}
